prompt: Does the Local Offer include information about resolving disagreements with the school or local authority?
completion: Yes, the Local Offer is required to include arrangements for resolving disagreements and for mediation, as well as details about making complaints. This means families can find that information alongside details of educational, health, and social care provision. It is intended to give a comprehensive and transparent picture of available support and routes for redress.

prompt: What role do schools play in developing the Local Offer?
completion: Schools are expected to co-operate with the local authority to help it develop and publish an authority-wide description of special educational provision available in schools. They also have the opportunity to take part in regular reviews of the Local Offer to identify gaps in provision. This collaborative role helps ensure the offer reflects the real needs of local children, young people, and families.

prompt: Can schools commission their own services for pupils with SEND, such as speech and language therapy?
completion: The guidance acknowledges that some schools act as commissioners of services for students with SEN or disabilities in their own right, which can include speech and language therapy, pastoral care, or counselling. Joint commissioning arrangements are expected to reflect and support this local commissioning activity. The aim is to ensure that school-commissioned services deliver positive outcomes for children and young people.

prompt: What duty does the local authority have to integrate education, health, and social care services?
completion: Under Section 25 of the Children and Families Act 2014, local authorities have a duty to ensure integration between educational, health, and social care provision where this would promote wellbeing and improve the quality of provision. Education, health, and social care partners also have a reciprocal duty to co-operate with the local authority on planning, commissioning, and reviewing services. Schools contribute by feeding into the Local Offer and wider strategic planning.

prompt: What outcomes should schools be aiming for when supporting pupils with SEND?
completion: The Code describes the expectation that all children and young people receive an education enabling them to achieve the best possible educational and other outcomes. This includes becoming confident, able to communicate their own views, and ready to make a successful transition into adulthood, whether into employment, further education, or training. High aspirations and expectations are central to this vision.

prompt: I have a pupil who seems to be struggling but hasn't been formally identified with SEN. What does the Code say about this?
completion: The Code describes identifying the needs of children and young people as one of the core principles guiding all professionals. A child has SEN if they have a learning difficulty or disability that calls for special educational provision to be made for them. Early identification is encouraged so that appropriate support can be put in place as soon as possible.

prompt: Does the SEND Code of Practice apply to pupils who have a disability but not a formal SEN identification?
completion: Children and young people with SEN may also have a disability under the Equality Act 2010, and the two frameworks can overlap. Where a child is covered by both SEN and disability legislation, reasonable adjustments and access arrangements are considered as part of SEN planning and review. The Local Offer also covers children and young people who do not have EHC plans.

prompt: What is the role of the SENCO under the new Code of Practice?
completion: The Code positions the SENCO in a strategic role, supporting teachers who are described as being at the heart of the new SEN support system. SENCOs help the school community understand and implement the reforms, working alongside strong leadership from head teachers and governors. They are also key contacts for families and for co-ordinating support across education, health, and social care.

prompt: What does 'preparing for adulthood' mean in the context of the SEND Code of Practice?
completion: Preparing for adulthood is a principle that runs through the Code from the earliest years, not just at transition points. It includes planning for paid employment, independent living, and a smooth move between children's and adults' services. The reforms introduced a stronger statutory focus on this area, recognising that good transition planning makes a real difference to young people's lives.

prompt: A parent is worried their child's needs won't be taken seriously because they don't have an EHC plan. What can I tell them?
completion: The Local Offer covers children and young people with SEN or disabilities whether or not they have an EHC plan, so support is not limited to those with a plan. The Code also describes SEN support in schools as a graduated response available to any pupil identified as having SEN. Families can explore what is available through the Local Offer and by speaking with the school's SENCO.

prompt: What does the Code say about inclusive education and removing barriers to learning?
completion: Focusing on inclusive practices and removing barriers to learning is listed as one of the core principles that all professionals working with children and young people with SEN or disabilities are guided to observe. The Code frames inclusion not as an add-on but as central to good-quality provision. This sits alongside high aspirations and a focus on outcomes that make a real difference to children's lives.

prompt: What happens if a school or local authority disagrees with a family about a child's SEND support?
completion: The Code includes a section on resolving disagreements, and the Local Offer is required to set out arrangements for disagreement resolution and mediation, as well as details about making complaints. Young people in further education also have the right to appeal to the First-tier Tribunal (SEN and Disability). These routes are intended to give families and young people a clear way to raise concerns.

prompt: Why does the Code emphasise culture change rather than just policy change?
completion: The foreword notes that for the reforms to have real impact, long-term culture change is needed alongside policy, including a confident and skilled workforce and a genuine voice for children and young people with SEN and disabilities. Policy alone cannot shift how schools and services respond day to day. The Code is therefore designed to support a whole-school shift in attitudes and practice, not just compliance with new rules.

prompt: Why does Ofsted care so much about whether I know my SEND pupils well enough?
completion: The Ofsted research found that secure knowledge of individual pupils supported timely and accurate identification of their needs. Where staff did not know pupils well, there was a negative impact on learning and development. Pupils without EHC plans and those less known to their SENCo were particularly affected.

prompt: My TA takes a child with autism out of class for interventions most mornings. Is that a problem?
completion: The research raises concerns about pupils spending significant curriculum time away from class, as this can mean missing whole areas of learning and reduce access to high-quality teaching. There are also potential social exclusion risks when pupils are regularly withdrawn. Balancing intervention time with full curriculum access is worth reviewing.

prompt: Some parents seem happy with the TA support but I'm worried about over-reliance. Am I right to be concerned?
completion: The research did find that in a small number of cases pupils had become over-reliant on their TAs, which can affect the development of independence. Most parents were positive about TA support, so the concern is understandable but not universal. Monitoring how independently a pupil can work is a reasonable step.

prompt: What subject knowledge do TAs actually need to run interventions effectively?
completion: The research highlights that TAs delivering intervention activities need strong subject and curriculum knowledge to teach pupils who have not yet mastered the intended curriculum. Without this, there is a risk that intervention content is poorly matched to the pupil's actual starting point. Robust subject-specific training for TAs is described as an important element of effective support.

prompt: A parent told me they feel left out of decisions about their child's SEND support. What does the research say about this?
completion: The research found that co-production mechanisms were often in place but not always implemented meaningfully, leaving some parents feeling uninformed about their child's learning. Some parents were not given opportunities to contribute to support plans or comment on targets. Where this happened, the graduated approach was effectively absent.

prompt: Does my child need a written support plan, or is verbal communication with the teacher enough?
completion: The research noted that some pupils did not have written support plans at all, and this meant the graduated approach was not in place. Without a written plan, parents also lose the opportunity to co-produce and review provision. A written plan helps ensure everyone is working from the same shared understanding.

prompt: How important is the SENCo's relationship with parents, really?
completion: The research describes SENCos as fulfilling a crucial intermediary role between external agencies, schools and families. Strong and trusting relationships between SENCos and parents were found to facilitate this role effectively. Parents in schools with positive SENCo relationships were more likely to feel confident about the school's broader approach to inclusion.

prompt: Our SENCo is also a full-time class teacher. Is that normal and does it matter?
completion: The research found that some SENCos were carrying out their role alongside a full-time teaching commitment, which it suggests indicates the role was not strongly prioritised in those schools. SENCos in this position reported not having enough time for their responsibilities or for continuing professional development. This can constrain how effectively they support pupils and families.

prompt: Why does it take so long to get an EHC plan assessment? It feels like the system is failing my pupil.
completion: The research acknowledges that long waiting times and high levels of bureaucracy around EHC plan assessments were a common frustration for SENCos, families and schools. The report notes that pupils lose too much time in appropriately planned education when identification is delayed. Local authority ambitions for collaboration did not always translate into smoother experiences in practice.

prompt: A child in my class has dyslexia and seems to be falling further behind despite interventions. What might be going wrong?
completion: The research found that some pupils were taught curriculum content they could not easily access because foundational knowledge and skills had not been secured first. If prior learning gaps are not addressed, understanding can widen over time rather than close. Checking whether the curriculum is properly sequenced to the pupil's actual starting point may help.

prompt: What does 'pupil-centred' actually mean in practice for SEND support?
completion: The research describes a pupil-centred approach as building a secure understanding of each child's needs, strengths and starting points. It also involves maintaining high expectations, fostering positive relationships with staff and peers, and supporting confidence and independence. Focusing on strengths alongside needs was a feature of schools where this worked well.

prompt: My child has ADHD and the school says they're getting support, but I don't know what that actually looks like. What should I expect?
completion: The research found that some parents were not given sufficient information about their child's learning and development, which is a recognised gap. Schools that worked well used a range of formal and informal communication channels to keep families informed and involved. Asking the SENCo for a written summary of current provision and targets is a reasonable starting point.

prompt: How do multi-agency services fit into what the school provides for SEND pupils?
completion: The research describes multi-agency input as complementing school provision when specialist support is required, and families and staff generally valued this. However, some pupils were not receiving adequate help even when external services were involved. Timely access to the full range of practitioners was identified as important for keeping support plans current and needs met.

prompt: Is it okay for a pupil with speech and language needs to miss literacy lessons for SALT sessions?
completion: The research raises concerns about pupils missing entire chunks of the curriculum due to withdrawal for intervention, noting this reduces the ambition of the curriculum they receive. Balancing specialist input with access to core learning is a genuine tension the research highlights. It is worth exploring whether sessions can be timed to minimise curriculum loss.

prompt: What does the research say about high expectations for pupils with SEND?
completion: The research identifies high expectations as an important feature of effective practice for pupils with SEND. Schools that held high expectations also tended to focus on pupils' strengths to build confidence and independence. Where expectations were lower, pupils were at greater risk of being offered a curriculum with less ambition than their peers.

prompt: Some families in our school pay privately for speech therapy because NHS waiting lists are so long. Is this a known issue?
completion: The research does acknowledge this, noting that some families felt the need to commission or pay for services themselves due to a lack of timely provision from external services. This was seen as highlighting the importance of a full range of multi-agency expertise being available. It reflects a wider pattern of uneven access to specialist support across the system.

prompt: How can I make sure a new pupil with SEND settles in well at transition?
completion: The research highlights strong communication at transition points as an important feature of effective SEND support. Gathering information from the previous setting and from the family early on helps build the secure understanding of the pupil that underpins good provision. Access to quiet spaces was also noted as a helpful adjustment during settling-in periods.

prompt: What does the research say about social inclusion for pupils with SEND in mainstream schools?
completion: The research found that ensuring pupils had positive relationships with staff and felt included with peers was a feature of some schools' approach. However, the amount of time spent outside class in small-group or individual interventions raised social exclusion concerns for some parents. Withdrawal arrangements are worth reviewing with inclusion in mind.

prompt: A parent wants to be more involved in setting targets for their child with Down syndrome. How does the research support this?
completion: The research describes co-production with parents as central to effective SEND support, with parents sharing information that helps schools identify needs more accurately. Some parents in the study were not given opportunities to input into support plans or comment on targets, which was seen as a gap. Inviting a parent to contribute to target-setting is consistent with the graduated approach.

prompt: Our local authority says it has a strategy for joined-up working, but in practice nothing seems to change. Why?
completion: The research found that both local authorities studied had strong ambitions for multi-agency collaboration, but this did not always translate into improved practice or positive experiences for schools and families. Challenges such as long waiting times and bureaucracy persisted despite strategic intentions. The gap between policy ambition and day-to-day reality is a recognised theme in the findings.

prompt: A child with social, emotional and mental health needs keeps being sent out of class. Could this be making things worse?
completion: The research suggests that regular withdrawal from class can contribute to social exclusion and may reduce a pupil's sense of belonging with peers. For pupils with social and emotional needs, positive relationships with staff and inclusion with classmates were identified as particularly important. Reviewing whether in-class adjustments could reduce the need for removal is worth considering.

prompt: How can I tell if a child in my nursery is stammering or just going through a normal phase of disfluency?
completion: Early years practitioners and parents can reliably identify stammering, though it can vary between home and nursery settings. Talking with parents or caregivers is therefore a vital part of building a full picture. Any parental concern about stammering is worth taking seriously, and the WellComm tool offers helpful guidance on when to refer to a Speech and Language Therapist.

prompt: Are there any validated tools I can use to screen for stammering in my early years setting?
completion: The rapid evidence review found no validated or reliable measures currently available for early years practitioners to formally identify stammering. Practitioner and parent observation remains the main approach at this stage. The WellComm tool can help guide decisions about when a referral to a Speech and Language Therapist is appropriate.

prompt: What strategies can I use in the classroom to support a child who stammers?
completion: Evidence for universal or targeted stammering interventions in early years is limited, though strategies drawn from specialist literature may help reduce the risk of stammering persisting and lessen any negative impact on the child. Creating a calm, unhurried communication environment is a commonly described approach. Specialist Speech and Language Therapist input is important where concerns are ongoing.

prompt: How do I know if a child's speech sound difficulties are serious enough to refer to a speech and language therapist?
completion: Practitioners can consider how intelligible the child is to others, whether they are producing age-appropriate sounds, and whether speech difficulties are affecting their confidence or participation. Tools such as the Intelligibility in Context Scale and speech milestones can support this thinking. Where a Speech Sound Disorder is identified, an individualised programme planned or overseen by a Speech and Language Therapist is required.

prompt: What speech assessment tools are available for children in the early years?
completion: Tools including the Intelligibility in Context Scale, SpeechLink, and the Speech Participation and Activity Assessment of Children are available, alongside speech milestone guidance. The review notes that all of these require further validation for UK early years contexts. Two phonological awareness assessments, PIPA and NAPA, can help identify whether a child may benefit from targeted phonological awareness support.

prompt: What interventions have good evidence for supporting children with speech sound difficulties?
completion: Phonological Awareness intervention and Recasting are two targeted approaches supported by moderate and good evidence respectively for children with Speech Sound Disorders. Additional techniques such as auditory bombardment and phonological contrast activities may also be beneficial. Any programme for a child with a Speech Sound Disorder is planned and delivered, or overseen, by a Speech and Language Therapist.

prompt: Which language assessment tools are most reliable for identifying language needs in young children?
completion: The review identified fourteen language tools with reported psychometric properties, with the British Picture Vocabulary Scales, Infant/Reception Language Link, Language Screen, and Sure Start Language Measure showing the strongest properties. No single tool captures all needs or age ranges, so using more than one tool together is described as essential. Speech and Language Therapist input remains an important part of identifying language needs in settings.

prompt: Can WellComm be used as a standalone tool to assess language development?
completion: WellComm supports collaborative practice and covers a wide age range, making it a useful tool in early years settings. However, the review notes that its psychometric properties require further evaluation. It works best as part of a broader approach that includes other tools and Speech and Language Therapist involvement rather than as a standalone measure.

prompt: What language intervention programmes have the strongest evidence for early years children?
completion: Six programmes showed good evidence of effectiveness: NELI, NELI Pre-school, Developing Talkers, Educator-Implemented Storybook Vocabulary Intervention, Happy Talk, and Talk Boost. Effective approaches consistently emphasised language-rich environments through responsive interaction, dialogic reading, explicit instruction, modelling, and repetition. Universal strategies alongside targeted support are described as essential rather than targeted support alone.

prompt: How important is it to involve parents when supporting a child's speech and language development?
completion: The review places children and their parents or caregivers at the centre of all collaborative support models. Involving families increases parental confidence in supporting their child at home, strengthens relationships, and improves engagement with wider services. Sharing knowledge with parents and caregivers is highlighted as a key feature of successful working together across the children's workforce.

prompt: What does good collaborative working between early years settings and speech and language therapists look like?
completion: Successful collaboration involves strategic leadership from senior Speech and Language Therapists, school leaders, and local authority stakeholders, alongside open communication and cross-professional networking. Hands-on collaboration promotes shared skills, boosts practitioner confidence, and improves accuracy in identifying needs. These integrated approaches lead to improved speech and language outcomes for children and earlier access to support.

prompt: What are the main barriers to effective joint working between early years settings and specialist services?
completion: The review identifies barriers including limited practitioner confidence and knowledge, insufficient training, and challenges around resources and sustainability. Relationships and opportunities for regular communication between professionals are also highlighted as important enablers that, when absent, can hinder collaboration. Addressing these factors is described as central to making tiered support models work effectively in practice.

prompt: How should I approach speech and language assessment for a bilingual child in my setting?
completion: Assessment tools alone are insufficient for bilingual children, and practitioners benefit from training to interpret results alongside contextual factors. The review emphasises that a holistic approach is particularly important, as many tools lack validation for bilingual populations. Speech and Language Therapist input and information from families about the child's home language are especially valuable in this context.

prompt: Are there specific interventions that work well for bilingual children with speech difficulties?
completion: The review notes that evidence on interventions specifically for bilingual children's speech is limited, though general speech approaches may still be applicable. Practitioners are encouraged to consider the child's home language as part of any support plan. Specialist Speech and Language Therapist involvement is particularly important when working with bilingual children who have identified speech needs.

prompt: What does the evidence say about supporting bilingual children who stammer?
completion: The review acknowledges that stammering in bilingual children requires careful consideration, as variability between languages and settings can complicate identification. Discussion with parents and caregivers about what they observe at home is described as vital. Referral to a Speech and Language Therapist with experience of both stammering and bilingualism is the most appropriate step where concerns arise.

prompt: What role does phonological awareness play in supporting children with speech difficulties?
completion: Phonological awareness intervention is one of two targeted approaches supported by moderate evidence for children with Speech Sound Disorders. Assessments such as PIPA and NAPA can help identify whether a child may benefit from this type of targeted support. Activities that develop awareness of sounds in words are a recognised component of effective speech intervention in the early years.

prompt: How can I create a language-rich environment in my early years setting?
completion: The review describes language-rich environments as consistently central to effective language support, built through responsive interaction, dialogic reading, explicit instruction, modelling, and repetition. These universal strategies are described as essential alongside any targeted intervention rather than as an alternative to it. Practitioners who receive training in these approaches are better placed to embed them meaningfully across the day.

prompt: What training do early years practitioners need to support children with speech and language needs?
completion: The review highlights that training is essential for practitioners to interpret assessment results accurately and consider contextual factors, particularly for bilingual children. Training also supports confidence in delivering targeted interventions and knowing when and how to refer to specialist services. Collaborative models that include ongoing knowledge-sharing between practitioners and Speech and Language Therapists are described as especially effective.

prompt: How does leadership affect the quality of speech and language support in early years settings?
completion: Strategic leadership involving senior Speech and Language Therapists, early years leaders, and local authority stakeholders is identified as an essential feature of successful collaborative support. Strong leadership enables consistent implementation of tiered models and supports sustainable use of specialist resources. The case studies in the review illustrate how leadership shapes both the ambition and the day-to-day delivery of speech and language support.

prompt: What is a tiered model of speech and language support and why does it matter?
completion: A tiered model organises support across universal, targeted, and specialist levels, with each tier building on the last to meet a range of needs efficiently. The review describes collaborative, tiered models developed across the children's workforce as the most effective and equitable way to deliver speech and language support. These models place children and families at the centre and aim to ensure timely, high-quality provision.

prompt: How can early years settings monitor the progress of children receiving speech and language support?
completion: The case studies highlight data collection and progress monitoring as key features of effective collaborative working in early years settings. Gathering information about children systematically helps practitioners and Speech and Language Therapists review whether support is having the intended impact. Shared tracking across the team, including with parents and caregivers, supports timely adjustments to provision.

prompt: What is stammering and how is it different from other speech difficulties?
completion: Stammering is characterised by whole and part-word repetitions, prolongations, and blocking of sounds. It may also involve physical tension, extra movements, or avoidance of certain words or speaking situations. This distinguishes it from speech sound difficulties, which relate to producing consonants and vowels accurately.

prompt: What age range does this early years review cover for speech and language needs?
completion: The rapid review focuses on children aged 0 to 5 years. It covers stammering, speech sound difficulties, and language development within that age range. Children with autism-related communication needs are outside the scope of this particular review.

prompt: Does this review cover children with autism who have communication difficulties?
completion: This review does not address the communication needs of children with autism or pragmatic and social communication difficulties. A separate parallel review was planned to cover those areas. The decision also helped keep the scope manageable within the available time and resources.

prompt: What tools are available for early years staff to identify speech and language needs?
completion: The review identified 22 tools that non-specialist early years staff can use to identify stammering, speech, and language needs. These were evaluated for reliability, validity, usability, and accuracy. The findings help practitioners decide which tool best fits their setting and the level of support a child may require.

prompt: Can a nursery practitioner use these identification tools without specialist training?
completion: The review specifically focused on tools that can be used by early years educators rather than specialists. Inclusion criteria required that tools were usable by non-specialist staff in early years settings. This means many of the identified tools are designed with the general early years workforce in mind.

prompt: What is the WellComm tool and is it included in the review?
completion: WellComm is one of six tools prespecified by the Department for Education and automatically included in the review. It was assessed alongside tools such as Language Screen, ELIM, and Infant Language Link. The review examined its psychometric properties and suitability for early years settings.

prompt: How were the identification tools assessed for quality in this review?
completion: Each tool was appraised for psychometric properties such as reliability, validity, specificity, and usability. Researchers sourced manuals and primary papers describing tool development, then searched databases for any later evaluations. Authors were also contacted directly to check whether further published evidence existed.

prompt: How can identification tools help decide whether a child needs specialist referral?
completion: The review explored how tools can guide decisions about universal, targeted, or specialist support. Certain profiles of need identified through these tools may indicate that immediate specialist referral is appropriate. The findings aim to help practitioners match the level of support to each child's individual profile.

prompt: Were any stammering interventions found that early years practitioners could deliver?
completion: The review found no suitable studies on stammering interventions at universal or targeted levels for early years practitioners. This is an important gap in the current evidence base. The review did identify 25 studies on broader speech and language interventions that practitioners can draw on.

prompt: What is the TIDieR framework and why was it used in this review?
completion: The Template for Intervention Description and Replication (TIDieR) framework was used to describe each intervention's components, delivery method, and rationale in a consistent way. This makes it easier to compare interventions and understand what is actually involved in delivering them. It supports practitioners in judging whether an intervention fits their setting.

prompt: How was the effectiveness of speech and language interventions measured in the review?
completion: Effect sizes were calculated using Hedges g and then interpreted using Education Endowment Foundation guidance, which expresses impact as months of progress. Study quality was also assessed using nine indicators including fidelity, blinding, and validity. This combination gives a rounded picture of both how well a study was conducted and how much difference an intervention made.

prompt: Can techniques from specialist stammering interventions be adapted for use in nursery settings?
completion: The review examined specialist intervention techniques to identify strategies that could be safely adapted for universal or targeted use by non-specialists. This analysis was included precisely because no practitioner-delivered stammering studies were found. It offers a practical bridge between specialist knowledge and everyday early years practice.

prompt: Who are the professionals involved in collaborative working to support early years SLCN?
completion: The review explored collaboration between early years settings and professionals including speech and language therapists, health visitors, educational psychologists, and parents. Effective collaboration was found to involve training, knowledge exchange, and flexible intervention pathways. Parents are highlighted as key partners alongside professional services.

prompt: What frameworks were used to analyse the case studies in the working together section?
completion: Case studies were analysed using the Theoretical Domains Framework and the COM-B model. These frameworks help identify the factors that enable or hinder effective collaboration in practice. Using them alongside literature review findings gave a richer understanding of how working together actually functions in early years contexts.

prompt: How many academic papers and grey literature sources were found on working together?
completion: The working together search identified nine peer-reviewed academic papers and five grey literature sources. These were supplemented by qualitative case studies to deepen understanding of collaborative practice. Together they highlight key components of effective multi-professional and family collaboration in early years settings.

prompt: What does 'language' mean in the context of this review?
completion: In this review, language refers to the understanding and use of words, sentences, and grammatical markers such as '-ing' and '-ed' to share meaning. This is distinct from speech, which concerns the production of individual sounds. Both areas are covered separately within the review's scope.

prompt: What is the difference between speech and language difficulties in young children?
completion: Speech difficulties involve problems producing consonants and vowels accurately to convey a message. Language difficulties relate to understanding or using words, sentences, and grammar to share meaning. The review treats these as distinct areas, each with its own identification tools and intervention approaches.

prompt: Was this review commissioned by the government and when was it carried out?
completion: The rapid review was commissioned by the Department for Education and conducted between February and July 2024. Its aim was to identify the best available evidence to guide early years practitioners supporting children aged 0 to 5 with SLCN. The findings are intended to inform universal and targeted support provision.

prompt: Is the Every Child a Talker programme included in the review's assessment tools?
completion: Every Child a Talker (ECAT) is one of the six tools prespecified by the Department for Education and automatically included in the review. It was appraised alongside other tools for its psychometric properties and suitability for non-specialist use. The review considered how it can support formative assessment decisions in early years settings.

prompt: How were the search strategies designed to find relevant studies for this review?
completion: Each work package used a tailored multi-source search strategy combining database searches, web reviews, grey literature, and expert input. Databases searched included Embase, Scopus, and ERIC, using both indexing terms and free-text searching. The approach was designed for rapid retrieval while still capturing the large majority of relevant evidence.

prompt: What are the limitations of a rapid review compared to a full systematic review?
completion: Rapid review methods provide an overview and synthesis of available evidence but do not guarantee a fully comprehensive identification of all relevant studies. The approach was chosen to meet the time and resource constraints of the commission. The review team is confident the searches captured the large majority of relevant and widely used evidence.

prompt: Can a headteacher suspend a pupil with ADHD for behaviour linked to their condition?
completion: The guidance asks headteachers to consider relevant factors before deciding to exclude, which would include a pupil's SEND needs. Suspension is described as a last resort, used only after other strategies have been exhausted. Equalities law is also flagged as an area schools are expected to consider alongside this guidance.

prompt: What counts as alternative provision for a pupil with autism who has been suspended?
completion: Alternative provision means suitable full-time education arranged from the sixth school day of a suspension, or earlier. It can include PRUs, alternative provision academies, hospital schools, and a range of other settings. The word 'suitable' matters here, as provision would need to meet the pupil's individual needs.

prompt: A parent of a child with dyslexia wants to challenge a permanent exclusion. What process is available to them?
completion: The governing board first considers whether the pupil should be reinstated, and the parent has the right to attend and make representations. If the parent remains dissatisfied, they can request an Independent Review Panel (IRP). A SEN expert can be requested to attend the IRP, which is particularly relevant where SEND is a factor.

prompt: What is the role of the SEN expert at an independent review panel?
completion: The SEN expert attends the IRP to provide impartial advice on how SEND may be relevant to the circumstances of the exclusion. Their role is advisory rather than advocacy for either party. This helps the panel consider whether SEND needs were properly taken into account before the exclusion decision was made.

prompt: Is there a 'no exclusion' policy allowed for pupils with emotional and behavioural difficulties?
completion: The guidance is clear that schools and local authorities are not expected to adopt a blanket 'no exclusion' policy. Such a policy can create safeguarding risks and may prevent a pupil from accessing alternative provision that better meets their needs. The aim is instead to build environments where exclusion simply becomes unnecessary.

prompt: My pupil has a hearing impairment and was excluded. Who counts as their 'parent' for the purposes of the exclusion process?
completion: The guidance uses a broad definition of parent drawn from the Education Act 1996. It includes birth parents, anyone with parental responsibility, and anyone who has care of the child, such as a foster carer. Where a local authority holds a care order, it is also included within that definition.

prompt: Can a pupil with a visual impairment attend a governing board exclusion review remotely?
completion: The guidance does allow for governing board meetings and IRPs to be held via remote access, such as a live video link. Annex A sets out key principles for how those meetings should be conducted. Accessibility considerations for a pupil with a visual impairment would be an important practical factor to address when arranging such a meeting.

prompt: How many days of suspension can a pupil with SEMH needs receive before the governing board must meet?
completion: The governing board's duty to consider an exclusion is set out in Part seven of the guidance, with specific triggers based on the length or cumulative nature of the suspension. The guidance does not apply different thresholds for pupils with SEND, though their needs are a relevant factor throughout the process.

prompt: Does this guidance apply to a pupil with Down syndrome attending a special school?
completion: The guidance applies to all maintained schools, including special schools, so it would cover a pupil attending a maintained special school. It applies to all pupils regardless of age or SEND status, except where age is specifically referenced. Independent special schools fall outside the scope of this guidance.

prompt: A teacher is worried that excluding a pupil with anxiety will make things worse. What does the guidance say about this?
completion: That concern is understandable, and the guidance reflects a similar caution by positioning exclusion as a last resort. Headteachers are expected to consider relevant factors before deciding to exclude, and the guidance points to mental health guidance as a companion resource. Alternative provision is also framed as a way to keep a pupil engaged in education, not simply as a punishment.

prompt: What happens to a permanently excluded pupil's place on the school roll if they have an EHCP?
completion: Part eight of the guidance covers the governing board's duty to remove a permanently excluded pupil's name from the school register. The guidance notes that SEND and equalities considerations are relevant throughout the exclusion process. For pupils with an EHCP, the local authority's responsibilities around provision would continue to apply separately.

prompt: Can a managed move be used instead of exclusion for a pupil with speech and language needs?
completion: The guidance describes managed moves as a behavioural strategy that can help prevent a suspension or permanent exclusion. They are presented as one of the tools available to headteachers alongside off-site direction. For a pupil with speech and language needs, any move would need to consider whether the receiving setting can meet those needs.

prompt: Who is responsible for arranging education for a suspended pupil with cerebral palsy from day six onwards?
completion: Part six of the guidance sets out that the governing board and local authority share duties to arrange suitable full-time education from the sixth school day of a suspension. The provision arranged would need to be appropriate to the pupil's needs. For a pupil with complex physical needs, accessibility of the provision would be a key consideration.

prompt: Does the guidance cover pupils with SEND who are directed off-site to improve their behaviour?
completion: The guidance does include off-site direction as one of the behavioural strategies covered alongside suspensions and permanent exclusions. It is described as a tool to help prevent escalation to formal exclusion. The guidance does not set out different rules for pupils with SEND in this context, though their needs remain a relevant consideration.

prompt: A parent of a child with dyscalculia says the school 'must' reinstate their child after the IRP. Is that right?
completion: An IRP cannot itself direct reinstatement, but Part twelve explains that the governing board has a duty to reconsider reinstatement following a review. The IRP can recommend reinstatement or recommend that the exclusion decision be reconsidered. The governing board then makes the final decision, though financial readjustment may follow if it does not reinstate.

prompt: What does 'must have regard to' mean in the context of this guidance for a pupil with dyspraxia?
completion: The guidance explains that 'must' refers to legal requirements, while 'must have regard to' means the statutory guidance cannot simply be ignored. Decision-makers are expected to consider it carefully, though they may depart from it if there is good reason. This distinction matters when a pupil's SEND needs are relevant to an exclusion decision.

prompt: Is a Virtual School Head involved when a looked-after child with SEMH needs is excluded?
completion: The guidance includes the Virtual School Head (VSH) among the key roles in the independent review process. The VSH has a specific interest in the education of looked-after children and would be expected to contribute relevant knowledge at an IRP. Their involvement reflects the additional vulnerability of children in care who face exclusion.

prompt: Can a pupil with selective mutism be excluded for not following instructions?
completion: The guidance asks headteachers to consider relevant factors before deciding to exclude, and a pupil's SEND needs would be central to that consideration. Excluding a pupil for behaviour that is a direct manifestation of their condition raises significant equalities concerns, which the guidance flags as a related area. Other strategies would be expected to have been tried first.

prompt: Does this guidance apply from September 2024 for a pupil with an EHCP who was excluded in July 2024?
completion: The guidance states clearly that it applies to exclusions occurring on or after 1 September 2024. An exclusion that took place in July 2024 would therefore fall under the previous version of the guidance. The 2023 version would be the relevant document for that case.

prompt: What guidance should schools read alongside this document when considering exclusion for a pupil with mental health needs?
completion: The guidance specifically names 'Mental health and behaviour in schools' as one of the companion documents schools are expected to consider. It also points to the Behaviour in Schools guidance and Keeping Children Safe in Education as key references. Links to all supplementary guidance are provided in Annex B.

prompt: A teacher wants to know if this guidance applies to a pupil with SEND attending a 16-19 academy.
completion: The guidance explicitly excludes 16-19 academies and 16-19 free schools from its scope. Those settings have separate suspension and permanent exclusion procedures. A pupil with SEND attending a 16-19 academy would therefore not be covered by this particular document.

prompt: What are the Teachers' Standards and who do they apply to?
completion: The Teachers' Standards define the minimum level of practice expected from the point QTS is awarded. They apply to trainees working towards QTS, early career teachers completing statutory induction, and teachers in maintained schools covered by the 2012 appraisal regulations. Part Two on personal and professional conduct applies across all education sectors.

prompt: Do the Teachers' Standards apply to a teacher with QTLS status working in my school?
completion: Headteachers have the freedom to decide which standards to use when assessing a QTLS holder's performance. They can apply the Teachers' Standards, other Secretary of State standards, relevant professional standards, or a combination. The QTLS teacher is informed of the chosen standards before or at the start of each appraisal period.

prompt: How should I interpret the bullet points under each teaching standard?
completion: The bullets are an integral part of each standard and are designed to amplify its scope rather than act as separate standards in their own right. Those assessing teachers can use them to track progress, spot areas for development, or recognise excellent practice. They work as a lens on the main heading, not a standalone checklist.

prompt: Can the Teachers' Standards be used to assess a teacher accused of serious misconduct?
completion: Part Two of the Teachers' Standards, covering personal and professional conduct, is used by the Teaching Regulation Agency when assessing cases of serious misconduct. This applies regardless of the education sector in which the teacher works. The TRA has used this element as a reference point since 1 April 2012.

prompt: How are the Teachers' Standards relevant to an early career teacher at the end of induction?
completion: At the end of induction, an ECT's performance is assessed against the Teachers' Standards at a level reasonable for their setting and circumstances. The standards themselves do not add new expectations beyond those required for QTS. The judgement reflects whether the ECT has consolidated training and demonstrated the standards consistently over a sustained period.

prompt: I'm a newly qualified teacher — do the standards change as I become more experienced?
completion: The standards provide a basic framework from initial qualification onwards, but the depth and breadth expected grows as a teacher's career progresses. Headteachers and appraisers apply professional judgement about what is reasonable at each career stage. Self-evaluation and professional development are described as critical to improving practice throughout a teaching career.

prompt: When did the current Teachers' Standards come into effect?
completion: The revised Teachers' Standards came into effect on 1 September 2012, when they became the 'specified standards' under Schedule 2 of The Education (School Teachers' Qualifications) (England) Regulations 2003. Trainees who completed training after that date are assessed against these standards. The conduct elements had already been in use from 1 April 2012.

prompt: How should the standards be applied differently for a trainee teacher compared with an experienced teacher?
completion: The guidance is clear that the standards are applied in a way that is consistent with what could reasonably be expected at each stage. For trainees, assessment reflects what is reasonable prior to QTS; for experienced teachers, the depth and breadth expected is greater. Professional judgement from headteachers and appraisers is described as central to this process.

prompt: What is the Preamble in the Teachers' Standards document?
completion: The Preamble summarises the values and behaviours that all teachers are expected to demonstrate throughout their careers. To meet the standards fully, a teacher's practice is consistent with the Preamble as well as Part One and Part Two. It sits alongside the teaching and conduct standards rather than replacing them.

prompt: A parent is asking whether their child's teacher has to meet any formal standards — what can I tell them?
completion: All qualified teachers in maintained schools are expected to perform at the level set out in the Teachers' Standards, which define the minimum expected practice. Performance is assessed against these standards through the school's appraisal arrangements. The standards cover both the quality of teaching and personal and professional conduct.

prompt: Does a teacher with SEND responsibilities need to meet any additional standards beyond the Teachers' Standards?
completion: The Teachers' Standards apply to all teachers, including those with SEND responsibilities, and are applied in a way that reflects the teacher's role and context. The guidance describes how depth and breadth of knowledge grow with career stage and setting. Additional SEND-specific expectations would come from separate statutory frameworks such as the SEND Code of Practice rather than the Teachers' Standards themselves.

prompt: How do the Teachers' Standards relate to supporting pupils with autism in my classroom?
completion: The Teachers' Standards set out the minimum expected practice for all teachers, which includes adapting teaching to meet the needs of all pupils. For a pupil with autism, this means the standards provide a baseline, while more detailed guidance on specific strategies comes from the SEND Code of Practice and school SEND policy. The standards are a starting point rather than a complete guide to autism-inclusive teaching.

prompt: Can the Teachers' Standards be used during appraisal to address concerns about how a teacher supports pupils with SEMH needs?
completion: The Teachers' Standards are used in maintained schools as the framework for appraisal, and headteachers apply professional judgement about what is reasonable for the teacher's role and context. Concerns about how a teacher supports pupils with SEMH needs could be explored through the relevant teaching standards. The appraisal process is also an opportunity to identify professional development in this area.

prompt: A trainee teacher is struggling to differentiate for pupils with dyslexia — how do the Teachers' Standards frame this?
completion: The Teachers' Standards set the minimum expected practice, and ITT providers assess trainees at a level consistent with what is reasonable before QTS is awarded. The bulleted subheadings within each standard can help identify where a trainee may benefit from further development, such as adapting teaching for pupils with dyslexia. This is framed as a developmental process rather than a pass-or-fail judgement.

prompt: What standards apply to a teacher who has QTLS but is teaching pupils with complex learning needs in my school?
completion: The headteacher has the freedom to decide which standards to use when appraising a QTLS holder, including the Teachers' Standards or other relevant professional standards. For a teacher working with pupils with complex learning needs, the chosen standards can reflect the specialist nature of the role. The QTLS teacher is informed of the applicable standards before or at the start of the appraisal period.

prompt: How do the Teachers' Standards support professional development for teachers working with pupils who have speech, language and communication needs?
completion: The guidance describes self-evaluation, reflection and professional development as critical to improving practice at all career stages. The standards identify key areas where teachers can assess their own practice and receive feedback from colleagues. For teachers supporting pupils with SLCN, this framework encourages growing depth of knowledge over time, though specific strategies would come from specialist guidance beyond the standards themselves.

prompt: Are there any standards that specifically mention pupils with special educational needs?
completion: The source text describes the Teachers' Standards as setting minimum expected practice across teaching and professional conduct, with Part One covering teaching standards. While the excerpt does not reproduce the full text of each standard, Standard 5 in Part One is widely known to address adaptive teaching for pupils with SEND. The standards are applied in a way that reflects the teacher's role and the needs of their pupils.

prompt: A parent wants to know if their child's teacher can be held accountable for not meeting the needs of their child with ADHD — what does the framework say?
completion: The Teachers' Standards provide the framework against which all teachers in maintained schools are appraised, and headteachers apply professional judgement about what is reasonable in the teacher's context. Concerns about how a pupil's needs are being met would typically be explored through the school's appraisal and SEND processes. The standards set a baseline, while the SEND Code of Practice provides more specific duties around meeting individual needs.

prompt: How should an ECT's progress be judged if they are working in a specialist SEND setting?
completion: The guidance is clear that the decision about whether an ECT has met the standards is based on what is reasonably expected in their relevant setting and circumstances. For an ECT in a specialist SEND setting, this means the judgement reflects the particular demands and context of that environment. The expectation is that the ECT has consolidated their training and demonstrates the standards consistently over a sustained period.

prompt: Can the Teachers' Standards be used to support a teacher who is finding it difficult to manage behaviour linked to a pupil's SEMH difficulties?
completion: The Teachers' Standards framework includes self-evaluation and professional development as tools for improving practice, and the bulleted subheadings within each standard can help identify areas for growth. A teacher finding behaviour management challenging in this context could use the standards as a starting point for a professional development conversation. More specific strategies for SEMH-related behaviour would come from specialist guidance and the school's SEND and behaviour policies.

prompt: Do the Teachers' Standards say anything about how teachers should reflect on their own practice when supporting diverse learners?
completion: The guidance describes appropriate self-evaluation and reflection as critical to improving practice at all career stages. The standards set out key areas where teachers can assess their own practice and receive feedback from colleagues. As careers progress, teachers are expected to extend the depth and breadth of their knowledge and skill, which naturally includes developing approaches for diverse learners.

prompt: Does the Equality Act 2010 cover disability discrimination for pupils in my school?
completion: Yes, disability is one of the protected characteristics covered by the Equality Act 2010. The Act makes discrimination unlawful in schools across admissions, education provision, and exclusions. This applies to all schools in England from 1 October 2010.

prompt: What is the reasonable adjustments duty and does it apply to my school?
completion: The reasonable adjustments duty requires schools to take steps to avoid putting disabled pupils at a substantial disadvantage compared with non-disabled peers. It applies to all schools covered by Chapter 1 of Part 6 of the Act. Since September 2012, the duty also extends to providing auxiliary aids and services.

prompt: What counts as a 'substantial disadvantage' when thinking about reasonable adjustments for a pupil with ADHD?
completion: The guidance describes 'substantial' as meaning more than minor or trivial, so a disadvantage that meaningfully affects a pupil's access to education would qualify. For a pupil with ADHD, difficulties concentrating during long assessments or following complex instructions could meet this threshold. Each situation is considered individually based on the effect of the disability.

prompt: Can the school charge parents for reasonable adjustments made for their disabled child?
completion: The guidance is clear that schools cannot charge pupils or their families for reasonable adjustments made to avoid substantial disadvantage. This applies whether the adjustment involves a change to practice, a physical feature, or an auxiliary aid. The cost of the adjustment is a factor in deciding what is reasonable, but it cannot be passed on to the family.

prompt: A pupil with autism struggles during school trips. Does the Equality Act cover school trips?
completion: School trips, both educational and non-educational, are explicitly covered under the Act's provisions on access to benefits, facilities, and services. A disabled pupil placed at a substantial disadvantage on a trip may be entitled to a reasonable adjustment. The school's duty applies whether the trip is curriculum-linked or purely recreational.

prompt: Is indirect discrimination relevant when a school policy on uniform affects a pupil with sensory processing difficulties?
completion: Indirect discrimination can arise when a policy that applies to everyone places pupils sharing a protected characteristic at a particular disadvantage. A uniform policy that causes significant difficulty for pupils with sensory processing difficulties could fall into this category. The school may need to show the policy is a proportionate means of achieving a legitimate aim.

prompt: Does the Equality Act protect a pupil who is being bullied because their sibling has a disability?
completion: The Act includes protection from discrimination by association, meaning a pupil can be protected because of a close relative's disability. Harassment related to a protected characteristic also covers conduct directed at someone connected to a disabled person. The guidance notes that victimisation provisions extend to actions of siblings in certain circumstances.

prompt: Can a school exclude a disabled pupil for behaviour that is linked to their disability?
completion: Excluding a pupil for behaviour that arises from their disability could constitute discrimination arising from disability under the Act. The guidance explains that unfavourable treatment connected to a disability is unlawful unless the school can objectively justify it. Whether reasonable adjustments were in place is also relevant to any assessment of the exclusion.

prompt: A parent is asking whether the school's admissions process can discriminate against their child who has dyslexia.
completion: The Act makes it unlawful for a school to discriminate against a pupil with a disability in its admissions arrangements, including the information provided, open events, and the criteria used. Dyslexia is likely to meet the Act's definition of disability if it has a substantial and long-term effect. A family concerned about admissions discrimination can pursue a claim through the relevant tribunal.

prompt: What auxiliary aids might a school be expected to provide for a pupil with a visual impairment?
completion: Since September 2012, the reasonable adjustments duty includes providing auxiliary aids and services. For a pupil with a visual impairment, examples might include large-print materials, screen-reading software, or specialist lighting. What is reasonable depends on factors such as cost, practicability, and how effectively the aid removes the disadvantage.

prompt: Does the Equality Act cover after-school clubs run by the school for pupils with physical disabilities?
completion: After-school clubs and extracurricular activities provided by the school are explicitly covered by the Act. A disabled pupil placed at a substantial disadvantage in accessing these activities may be entitled to a reasonable adjustment. The duty applies to the school as the responsible body, regardless of whether the activity is curricular.

prompt: A teacher wants to know if they can be personally liable for discriminating against a pupil with a learning disability.
completion: The guidance confirms that individual employees, including teachers, can be personally liable for acts of discrimination, harassment, or victimisation. This is separate from the school's own liability as the responsible body. Understanding this helps staff appreciate that the Act's protections are not just an institutional concern.

prompt: How does the school's duty to make reasonable adjustments interact with an EHCP?
completion: The guidance lists the special educational provision already being made under an EHCP as one factor in deciding what further reasonable adjustments are required. Provision in an EHCP does not automatically satisfy the reasonable adjustments duty, but it is taken into account. The two frameworks work alongside each other rather than one replacing the other.

prompt: Is it harassment under the Equality Act if a teacher makes repeated negative comments about a pupil's speech and language difficulties?
completion: Harassment under the Act covers unwanted conduct related to a protected characteristic that has the purpose or effect of violating a person's dignity or creating a hostile environment. Repeated negative comments about a pupil's communication difficulties could meet this definition if they relate to the pupil's disability. The guidance makes clear that harassment by school staff is unlawful.

prompt: Can a school justify not making an adjustment for a pupil with dyspraxia if it would be very expensive?
completion: Cost is one of several factors the guidance identifies when assessing whether a step is reasonable, alongside practicability and effectiveness. A very high cost relative to the school's resources could mean a particular adjustment is not required. However, cost alone is unlikely to justify inaction if lower-cost alternatives could still reduce the disadvantage.

prompt: Does the Equality Act apply to how a school handles tests and exams for pupils with disabilities?
completion: Tests, assessments, and exams are explicitly listed as areas covered by the Act's provisions on education and access to benefits. A disabled pupil placed at a substantial disadvantage in an assessment context may be entitled to a reasonable adjustment. Access arrangements such as extra time or a reader are common examples of how schools respond.

prompt: A parent is worried their child with mental health difficulties is being treated less favourably than other pupils. What protection does the Act offer?
completion: If a mental health condition has a substantial and long-term effect on daily activities, it is likely to be a disability under the Act. Direct discrimination occurs when a pupil is treated less favourably because of their disability without justification. Discrimination arising from disability can also apply where unfavourable treatment is connected to the effects of the condition.

prompt: Does the Equality Act cover careers education for pupils with disabilities?
completion: Careers education and information are listed among the areas covered by the Act's provisions on education and access to benefits, facilities, and services. A disabled pupil placed at a substantial disadvantage in accessing careers guidance may be entitled to a reasonable adjustment. This could include accessible formats or additional support during careers interviews.

prompt: Can a school take positive action to support pupils with SEND who are underrepresented in extracurricular activities?
completion: The Act permits positive action where a school reasonably believes that pupils sharing a protected characteristic are underrepresented or face disadvantage in a particular activity. Targeted encouragement or tailored support to increase participation in extracurricular activities could fall within this provision. Positive action is voluntary rather than mandatory, but it aligns with the public sector equality duty.

prompt: What happens if a parent believes the school has discriminated against their disabled child? Where can they take a claim?
completion: Disability discrimination claims relating to schools in England can be brought before the Special Educational Needs and Disability tribunal. The tribunal can make a range of orders if it upholds a claim. Time limits apply, so families benefit from seeking advice promptly after the issue arises.

prompt: Does the Equality Act protect a pupil with epilepsy from being excluded from science practicals on health and safety grounds?
completion: Health and safety is one factor the guidance acknowledges when assessing reasonable adjustments, but it cannot be used as a blanket reason to exclude a disabled pupil from activities. Excluding a pupil with epilepsy from science practicals without exploring adjustments could constitute discrimination arising from disability. A proportionate, evidence-based risk assessment is the appropriate starting point.

prompt: Why do neurodivergent pupils have worse outcomes than their peers?
completion: The report explains that structural barriers within the education system make it harder for some neurodivergent learners to access and benefit from schooling. These barriers are compounded by inequalities linked to ethnicity, gender, and socioeconomic position. Poor long-term outcomes—including higher rates of suspension, absence, and mental health need—are described as downstream consequences of those access barriers.

prompt: What does success actually look like for neurodivergent pupils according to this report?
completion: The report frames success not as a list of specific accommodations but as whether neurodivergent children genuinely feel they belong and are wanted in school. It also asks whether pupils are supported to achieve, attain, and prepare for adulthood. That sense of belonging is described as the real litmus test.

prompt: Do we need a diagnosis before we can put support in place for a child?
completion: The report is clear that support does not need to wait for a clinical diagnosis. Assessment tools in schools are intended to identify educational needs and foster strengths as early as possible, independent of any diagnostic process. The NDTFG was specifically asked to consider support that need not depend on a formal clinical label.

prompt: What kind of assessment tools does the report recommend schools use?
completion: The report calls for free, evidence-based, validated, and developmentally appropriate assessment tools that are easy to interpret and implement. These tools are intended to map directly onto deliverable education plans rather than screen for specific clinical conditions. The emphasis is on identifying needs and strengths quickly so support can follow without delay.

prompt: How should schools involve parents in supporting neurodivergent children?
completion: The report introduces the idea of a 'commitment to listen' to parents, carers, and neurodivergent young people, recognising settings that do this well. Parents and young people are described as having a meaningful role in both assessment and subsequent planning processes. The report also highlights the value of dialogue and information sharing across education, health, and social care.

prompt: What does the report say about partnerships between education, health, and social care?
completion: Recommendation Three calls for clear partnerships between education, health, and social care to support neurodivergent needs. The report describes dialogue and information sharing across these systems as central to a needs-led approach. Local population data from multiple services is also highlighted as important for planning and commissioning.

prompt: What professional development does the report recommend for teachers working with neurodivergent pupils?
completion: The report recommends high-quality professional development focused on strengths, needs, and support strategies for neurodivergent children. It suggests minimum standards within Initial Teacher Training and the Early Career Framework, alongside post-qualification development through NPQs. Crucially, training is described as needing to go beyond a handful of clinical conditions to cover common barriers to learning.

prompt: Should teacher training focus on specific diagnoses like ADHD or autism?
completion: The report advises moving beyond partial coverage of specific clinical conditions in teacher training. Instead, the focus is on the best evidence around common barriers to learning and participation that teachers encounter regardless of any formal label. This approach is intended to benefit a wider range of neurodivergent learners.

prompt: Why are so many neurodivergent pupils absent from school?
completion: The report notes that attendance rates are particularly low for neurodivergent children and young people. It suggests that improving attendance begins with understanding and addressing why a child is missing school, rather than relying on sanctions that may worsen anxiety or disengagement. Addressing the root cause is presented as more effective than punitive approaches.

prompt: Our school uses sanctions for poor attendance—is that the right approach for neurodivergent pupils?
completion: The report cautions that sanctions and policies focused on consequences may worsen anxiety or disengagement for neurodivergent pupils. Understanding why a child is missing school is described as the starting point for improving attendance. This reflects a broader call in the report for behaviour policies rooted in understanding, acceptance, and curiosity rather than punitive responses.

prompt: What does the report say about exclusions for neurodivergent pupils?
completion: The report highlights that suspension and exclusion rates are high for neurodivergent children and young people. Behaviour policies are described as needing to be rooted in an inclusive school culture that values understanding, acceptance, and curiosity. The report links high exclusion rates to the broader need for cultural and systems change in mainstream settings.

prompt: How should we adapt the learning environment for neurodivergent pupils?
completion: The report describes learning environments as needing to be accessible across physical, social, sensory, and communication dimensions. Behaviour policies are also highlighted as an important part of the environment, with the report calling for an inclusive school culture built on understanding and curiosity. National standards incorporated within Ofsted inspections are proposed as a way to embed these expectations.

prompt: Can we make adjustments to curriculum and assessment for neurodivergent pupils?
completion: The report recommends that schools be given the flexibility and incentive to adapt both curriculum and forms of assessment to make settings more inclusive by design. A useful principle offered is that if a change is vital for one child, it is likely beneficial for all and could become the new standard. This framing supports a universal design approach rather than one-off adjustments.

prompt: What does 'inclusive by design' mean in practice for our school?
completion: The report uses 'inclusive by design' to mean that adaptations to environment, curriculum, and assessment are built into standard practice rather than added on for individual pupils. It draws on the idea that what helps one neurodivergent learner often benefits the whole class. This approach is linked to a broader cultural shift away from parallel pathways toward genuinely inclusive mainstream settings.

prompt: How should funding for neurodivergent pupils be calculated?
completion: The report recommends increasing funding to schools to reflect the level of neurodivergent need within their population, rather than relying solely on diagnosis rates. A commissioning model is proposed that draws on local population data from education, health, and social care, including demographic factors such as ethnicity, gender, and socioeconomic position. Targeted additional funding is also described as important when specialist input is needed.

prompt: What happens when there is a disagreement between a family and the school about SEND support?
completion: The report recommends developing clear and effective local routes to resolution when challenges occur between families and settings. Having accessible local processes is presented as an important part of a fair and responsive system. This sits alongside the broader 'commitment to listen' to parents and young people described in Recommendation Two.

prompt: Is it enough to just add new initiatives on top of what we already do?
completion: The report is direct on this point: equity will not be achieved by pasting new initiatives onto an existing, under-resourced system. It also cautions against creating multiple parallel pathways for different groups of children. Instead, the report calls for both a cultural and a systems shift, describing these as inseparable.

prompt: How does the report suggest we use data to plan for neurodivergent pupils?
completion: The report describes a commissioning model that draws on data from education, health, and social care rather than diagnosis rates alone. Local population data recording ethnicity, gender, socioeconomic position, and other demographic characteristics is highlighted as essential. Better data collection, linkage, and long-term outcome tracking across public services are all part of the proposed accountability framework.

prompt: A parent is worried their child won't get support because they don't have a diagnosis yet. What can I tell them?
completion: It is understandable to feel anxious about this, and the report's position is reassuring: support is described as independent from any clinical diagnostic process. The NDTFG was specifically asked to consider what support need not depend on a formal label. Assessment tools in schools are intended to identify needs and strengths early, so that help can follow without waiting for a diagnosis.

prompt: Does Ofsted look at how schools support neurodivergent pupils?
completion: The report proposes that national standards for learning environments be incorporated within Ofsted inspections. This would mean that how well a school addresses physical, social, sensory, and communication barriers becomes part of the inspection framework. The report presents this as a way of embedding inclusive practice as an expectation rather than an optional extra.

prompt: Why does the report say we need both cultural and systems change?
completion: The report argues that culture and systems are interdependent: changing one without transforming the other is described as ineffective. A cultural shift means schools genuinely valuing and welcoming neurodivergent pupils, while a systems shift means funding, accountability, and commissioning structures that make inclusion sustainable. Both are presented as necessary conditions for neurodivergent children to feel they truly belong.

prompt: What is the SEND local offer and why does it matter for my pupils?
completion: The local offer sets out all education, health and social care provision available for children and young people with SEND in a local area. It aims to give families clear, accessible information about support and how to reach it. It also exists to make services more responsive by involving children, families and providers in its development.

prompt: Who is responsible for publishing the local offer in our area?
completion: The local authority holds responsibility for publishing the local offer. However, partner bodies across health and education are expected to cooperate in developing and reviewing it. The guidance frames this as a shared local area partnership effort rather than a task for one organisation alone.

prompt: Will Ofsted be judging our school during these thematic visits?
completion: These thematic visits are not school inspections and no judgement is formed about individual areas or settings. Findings will be gathered across a small number of local areas and summarised in a national report. Individual areas will be listed but findings will not be attributed to them.

prompt: Which children are covered by the scope of these thematic visits?
completion: The visits cover children and young people aged 0 to 25 who have SEND. This includes those with an Education, Health and Care plan and those receiving SEN support without a plan. The broad age range reflects the full span of SEND provision from early years through to young adulthood.

prompt: What are the four themes inspectors will look at during the 2026 visits?
completion: Inspectors will explore how the local area understands children's needs, how it oversees and delivers local offer services, how families and young people are involved in developing the offer, and what experiences children and families have when accessing support. Each theme looks at a different layer of the SEND system.

prompt: How might these visits affect the way our school works with the local authority on SEND?
completion: The visits are designed to generate insights that promote improvement across the sector, so findings may influence how local partnerships develop services over time.
